title: Twelve hours on the road
summary: A journalist asks a long-distance bus driver what he sees from the front seat that the passengers don't.

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: Bác Quang, you've driven this route for thirty-one years. Hanoi to Da Nang. Each way, eighteen hours.

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: What do you see, from the front seat, that the passengers don't?

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: What do you mean by 'see'? The road?

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: The people. Their journeys. What you notice that they think nobody notices.

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: Ah. Let me think about that question. Most passengers think the bus driver is watching the road. He is watching the road. But the mirror is right there, all night.

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: Tell me one thing you've seen in the mirror.

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: I'll tell you three. A woman, maybe forty-five, who cried for the first six hours, very quietly, every Friday for a year. Then suddenly, one Friday, she didn't. She slept like everyone else. I think she had stopped going to see whatever made her cry. I never asked. It wasn't my business.

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: Two young soldiers, brothers I think, who took it in turns to sleep so the other could sit awake the whole night. They wouldn't both sleep at the same time. I never understood why. Maybe their mother had told them, when you travel, one always watches. I have not seen them again.

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: A girl, eighteen or nineteen, who got on the bus in Hanoi with one bag and an empty face. By Vinh, she was on the phone, smiling. By Hue, she was helping an old woman across the aisle. By Da Nang, she walked off the bus a different person from the one who got on. I have seen this many times. The bus does something to people. We think a bus is for getting somewhere. But for some, it's for becoming someone.

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: Do the passengers ever talk to you?

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: Sometimes. Mostly at four in the morning, the dangerous hour. People say things to a bus driver they would not say to a friend. He is not in their life. He will not remember. Except — I do remember. Some of it. Names, no. The shape of a story, yes.

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: Everyone surprises me. The man who looks the toughest is the one who, halfway through, asks if I have a tissue. The quiet grandmother by the window is the one who, when we stopped for petrol, taught me the proper way to fold a banana leaf for sticky rice. I have stopped expecting people to match their faces.

body.speaker: Phong
body.text: If you had to give passengers one piece of advice —

body.speaker: Bác Quang
body.text: Don't sleep through Hue. The light there at five in the morning, in November, is the only reason I have driven this road for thirty-one and a half years.

annotations.meaningVi: gương (ở đây là gương chiếu hậu)
cứng rắn nhất, mạnh mẽ nhất
thay phiên nhau (làm việc gì đó theo lượt)
khuôn mặt trống rỗng, vô cảm — không biểu lộ cảm xúc
qua lối đi giữa hai hàng ghế
đến giữa chặng đường / nửa chừng
một người khác hẳn — dùng để chỉ sự thay đổi sâu sắc bên trong
hình hài / đại ý của một câu chuyện (không nhất thiết nhớ chi tiết)
trở thành một người (nào đó) — chỉ sự trưởng thành, biến đổi bản thân
giờ nguy hiểm — chỉ khoảng 4 giờ sáng khi người ta dễ bộc bạch thật lòng
không phải việc của tôi — cách diễn đạt lịch sự để nói không can thiệp
một lời khuyên (advice là danh từ không đếm được, luôn dùng 'a piece of advice')
đã thôi không còn mong đợi / đã bỏ kỳ vọng
ngủ qua (một sự kiện, địa điểm) — không thức dậy hoặc bỏ lỡ vì ngủ

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life experience and ongoing situations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + V3) có hai cách dùng quan trọng: (1) trải nghiệm trong đời — 'I have seen this many times' (Tôi đã thấy điều này nhiều lần); (2) hành động hoặc trạng thái bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp tục đến hiện tại, thường đi với 'for + khoảng thời gian' — 'I have driven this road for thirty-one and a half years' (Tôi đã lái con đường này được ba mươi mốt năm rưỡi).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect (have/has + past participle) is used for two key meanings: (1) life experience — 'I have seen this many times'; (2) a state or action that started in the past and continues now, often with 'for + period' — 'I have driven this road for thirty-one and a half years.'

cloze.template:
Phong: Bác Quang, you've driven this route for {{b1}} years. Hanoi to Da Nang. Each way, eighteen hours.
Bác Quang: Thirty-one and a half.
Phong: What do you see, from the front seat, that the passengers don't?
Bác Quang: What do you mean by 'see'? The road?
Phong: The people. Their journeys. What you notice that they think nobody notices.
Bác Quang: Ah. Let me think about that question. Most passengers think the bus driver is watching the road. He is watching the road. But the {{b2}} is right there, all night.
Phong: Tell me one thing you've seen in the mirror.
Bác Quang: I'll tell you three. A woman, maybe forty-five, who cried for the first six hours, very quietly, every {{b3}} for a year. Then suddenly, one Friday, she didn't. She slept like everyone else. I think she had stopped going to see whatever made her cry. I never asked. It wasn't my business.
Phong: And the second?
Bác Quang: Two young {{b4}}, {{b5}} I think, who took it in turns to sleep so the other could sit awake the whole night. They wouldn't both sleep at the same time. I never understood why. Maybe their mother had told them, when you travel, one always watches. I have not seen them again.
Phong: And the third?
Bác Quang: A girl, {{b6}} or nineteen, who got on the bus in Hanoi with one bag and an empty face. By Vinh, she was on the {{b7}}, {{b8}}. By Hue, she was helping an old woman across the aisle. By Da Nang, she walked off the bus a different person from the one who got on. I have seen this many times. The bus does something to people. We think a bus is for getting somewhere. But for some, it's for {{b9}} someone.
Phong: Do the passengers ever talk to you?
Bác Quang: Sometimes. Mostly at four in the morning, the {{b10}} hour. People say things to a bus driver they would not say to a friend. He is not in their life. He will not remember. Except — I do remember. Some of it. Names, no. The shape of a story, yes.
Phong: Has anyone surprised you?
Bác Quang: Everyone surprises me. The man who looks the toughest is the one who, halfway through, asks if I have a {{b11}}. The quiet grandmother by the window is the one who, when we stopped for petrol, taught me the proper way to fold a banana leaf for sticky rice. I have stopped expecting people to match their faces.
Phong: If you had to give passengers one piece of advice —
Bác Quang: Don't sleep through {{b12}}. The light there at five in the morning, in November, is the only reason I have driven this road for thirty-one and a half years.

blanks.explanation: 'You've driven this route for thirty-one years.' Bác Quang gently corrects: thirty-one and a half.
'The mirror is right there, all night.' That is where he sees what the passengers think no one sees.
She cried 'every Friday for a year' — until one Friday, she suddenly didn't.
Two young soldiers — they took turns to sleep so one was always awake.
He guesses they were brothers, from the way they behaved towards each other.
A girl, eighteen or nineteen, with one bag and an empty face.
By Vinh, she was on the phone, smiling — the first sign of life he saw on the journey.
She was smiling — the second small sign of change in her face.
'For some, it's for becoming someone.' The journey changes the passenger, not just their location.
Four in the morning — 'the dangerous hour'. The hour when secrets and honesty come out.
Halfway through, the tough-looking man asks for a tissue — the moment that broke the writer's expectation.
Don't sleep through Hue — the November dawn light there is, for Bác Quang, the reason for thirty-one and a half years on the road.

translationVi:
Phong: Bác Quang, bác đã lái tuyến này được ba mươi mốt năm. Hà Nội – Đà Nẵng. Mỗi chiều, mười tám tiếng.
Bác Quang: Ba mươi mốt năm rưỡi.
Phong: Từ ghế lái, bác thấy gì mà hành khách không thấy?
Bác Quang: Cháu nói 'thấy' là theo nghĩa gì? Đường á?
Phong: Con người ấy ạ. Hành trình của họ. Những gì bác để ý mà họ tưởng chẳng ai để ý.
Bác Quang: À. Để bác nghĩ về câu đó. Hầu hết hành khách nghĩ bác tài đang nhìn đường. Bác đang nhìn đường thật. Nhưng cái gương ở ngay đó, suốt đêm.
Phong: Bác kể cho cháu nghe một điều bác thấy trong gương đi.
Bác Quang: Bác kể ba điều. Một người phụ nữ, chắc bốn mươi lăm tuổi, đã khóc trong sáu tiếng đầu, rất khẽ, mỗi thứ Sáu suốt một năm. Rồi đột nhiên, một thứ Sáu, cô ấy không khóc nữa. Cô ấy ngủ như mọi người. Bác nghĩ cô ấy đã ngừng đi gặp cái thứ làm cô ấy khóc. Bác chưa bao giờ hỏi. Không phải việc của bác.
Phong: Còn điều thứ hai ạ?
Bác Quang: Hai chàng lính trẻ, bác đoán là hai anh em, thay phiên nhau ngủ để người kia ngồi thức trông suốt đêm. Họ không bao giờ ngủ cùng lúc. Bác không hiểu vì sao. Có thể mẹ họ đã dặn, khi đi xa, luôn phải có một người thức. Bác không gặp họ lần nào nữa.
Phong: Và điều thứ ba?
Bác Quang: Một cô gái, mười tám hay mười chín tuổi, lên xe ở Hà Nội với một cái túi và gương mặt trống rỗng. Đến Vinh, cô ấy đang gọi điện thoại, mỉm cười. Đến Huế, cô ấy đang giúp một bà cụ đi qua lối đi. Đến Đà Nẵng, cô ấy bước xuống xe như một người khác hẳn người vừa lên. Bác đã thấy chuyện này nhiều lần. Cái xe làm gì đó cho con người. Mình nghĩ xe khách là để đi đến đâu đó. Nhưng với một số người, nó là để trở thành ai đó.
Phong: Hành khách có bao giờ nói chuyện với bác không ạ?
Bác Quang: Thi thoảng. Hầu hết vào bốn giờ sáng, cái giờ nguy hiểm. Người ta nói với bác tài những điều họ sẽ không nói với bạn. Bác không có trong đời họ. Bác sẽ không nhớ. Trừ — bác có nhớ. Một số chuyện. Tên thì không. Hình hài của một câu chuyện, có.
Phong: Có ai từng làm bác bất ngờ không?
Bác Quang: Ai cũng làm bác bất ngờ. Người đàn ông trông cứng cỏi nhất lại là người, đến giữa đường, hỏi xin một tờ khăn giấy. Bà cụ ngồi yên bên cửa sổ lại là người, khi mình dừng đổ xăng, dạy bác cách gói lá chuối đúng cho xôi. Bác đã thôi mong người ta khớp với gương mặt của họ.
Phong: Nếu phải cho hành khách một lời khuyên —
Bác Quang: Đừng ngủ qua Huế. Ánh sáng ở đó lúc năm giờ sáng, tháng Mười Một, là lý do duy nhất khiến bác đã lái con đường này suốt ba mươi mốt năm rưỡi.

questions.prompt: How long has Bác Quang driven the Hanoi–Da Nang route?
questions.explanation: Phong says 'thirty-one years'; Bác Quang corrects gently: 'Thirty-one and a half.'

questions.prompt: What does Bác Quang say is the difference between watching the road and seeing the passengers?
options: He looks at the road, but the mirror lets him see passengers all night
Passengers face forward, so he never sees their faces
questions.explanation: 'But the mirror is right there, all night.'

questions.prompt: What was the story of the woman who cried for six hours?
options: She cried for years and never stopped
She cried every Friday for a year, then suddenly stopped — he thinks she had stopped going to see whatever made her cry
She always cried because she hated the bus
questions.explanation: 'I think she had stopped going to see whatever made her cry.'
questions.hint: Look at Bác Quang's first story.

questions.prompt: What was unusual about the two young soldiers?
options: They both refused to sleep at the same time — one always stayed awake
They got off in different cities
questions.explanation: 'They wouldn't both sleep at the same time… Maybe their mother had told them, when you travel, one always watches.'

questions.prompt: What happened to the eighteen-year-old girl during the journey?
options: She got on with an empty face; by Vinh she was smiling on the phone; by Hue she was helping an old woman; by Da Nang she was a different person
questions.explanation: The change is described as a slow transformation across the cities the bus passes through.
questions.hint: Look at Bác Quang's third story.

questions.prompt: What does Bác Quang mean by 'the bus is for becoming someone'?
options: The bus is for moving cargo
Some passengers use the long journey to change inside, not just to travel between places
questions.explanation: 'We think a bus is for getting somewhere. But for some, it's for becoming someone.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of the third story.

questions.prompt: When do passengers most often talk to him?
options: At midnight, when they get on
At four in the morning — 'the dangerous hour'
questions.explanation: 'Mostly at four in the morning, the dangerous hour.'
questions.hint: Look at the turn after 'Do the passengers ever talk to you?'

questions.prompt: Why do passengers say things to him they would not say to a friend?
options: He is not in their life and will not remember (although he does remember the shape of the story)
questions.explanation: 'He is not in their life. He will not remember. Except — I do remember. Some of it.'
questions.hint: Look at his explanation of why people open up.

questions.prompt: What surprised him about the 'toughest-looking' man?
options: He asked for a tissue halfway through the journey
He drove the bus for a while
questions.explanation: 'The man who looks the toughest is the one who, halfway through, asks if I have a tissue.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn beginning 'Everyone surprises me.'

questions.prompt: What advice does Bác Quang give passengers at the end?
options: Don't sleep through Hue — the November dawn light there is the reason he has driven this road for thirty-one and a half years
questions.explanation: 'Don't sleep through Hue. The light there at five in the morning, in November, is the only reason I have driven this road for thirty-one and a half years.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last turn.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Bác Quang says that for some passengers a bus is 'not for getting somewhere — it's for becoming someone'. Think of a long journey you have taken (a bus, a train, a flight, even a walk). What did you carry on at the start that you no longer carried by the end? What had the journey actually been for?
